nodes.name: Greek & Roman Mythology
nodes.description: Greek and Roman gods, heroes, and myths

items.prompt: In Greek mythology, who is the king of the Olympian gods, ruler of Mount Olympus, and god of the sky, thunder, and lightning?
items.explanation:
King of the Olympian gods in Greek mythology; Roman equivalent is Jupiter (or Jove)
Son of the Titans Cronus and Rhea; led his siblings to overthrow the Titans in the Titanomachy
Wields the thunderbolt as his primary weapon; the eagle and the oak are sacred to him
Famous for his many affairs with mortals and goddesses: fathered Heracles, Perseus, Apollo, Artemis, Athena, and dozens more
The Olympic Games were held in his honor at Olympia every four years
The great statue of Zeus at Olympia (by Pheidias, c. 430 BC) was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
items.cardFront: King of Olympian gods; sky, thunder, lightning

items.prompt: In Greek mythology, who is the god of the sea, earthquakes, and horses, who carries a trident and is brother to Zeus and Hades?
items.explanation:
God of the sea, earthquakes, and horses in Greek mythology; Roman equivalent is Neptune
One of three brothers who divided the world after overthrowing Cronus: Zeus got the sky, Poseidon the sea, Hades the underworld
His trident can shake the earth; he is called 'Earth-Shaker' in Homer
Created the horse as a gift to humans (or in a contest with Athena for Athens's patronage)
Lost the contest for patronage of Athens to Athena: he struck the ground and produced salt water; she grew an olive tree
Blind Polyphemus, the Cyclops blinded by Odysseus, is his son; Poseidon's wrath drives much of the *Odyssey*'s plot
items.cardFront: God of sea, earthquakes, horses; trident

items.prompt: In Greek mythology, who is the god of the underworld and the dead, whose realm shares his name and whose wife is Persephone?
items.explanation:
God and ruler of the underworld in Greek mythology; Roman equivalents are Pluto and Dis
One of three sons of Cronus; received the underworld as his domain after the Titanomachy
His name means 'the unseen one'; he possessed a helmet of invisibility
Kidnapped Persephone (daughter of Demeter) to be his queen; her annual return causes spring
The underworld contains the rivers Styx, Lethe, and Acheron; the dead drink from Lethe to forget their lives
Not a god of death per se (that is Thanatos); Hades simply rules the realm where all dead souls go
items.cardFront: Ruler of the underworld; Persephone's husband

items.prompt: In Greek mythology, who is the goddess of wisdom, warfare strategy, and crafts, said to have sprung fully-grown and armored from the forehead of Zeus?
items.explanation:
Goddess of wisdom, strategic warfare, and crafts in Greek mythology; Roman equivalent is Minerva
Born fully armed from Zeus's forehead after he swallowed her pregnant mother Metis
Patron goddess of Athens; competed with Poseidon for the city's patronage and won by gifting the olive tree
Depicted with helmet, spear, and the aegis (a shield bearing the Gorgon's head); her bird is the owl
Virgin goddess; never took a lover or consort among gods or mortals
The Parthenon (temple of Athena Parthenos, 'the Virgin') on the Athenian Acropolis was dedicated to her
items.cardFront: Goddess of wisdom, strategy, crafts; born from Zeus's forehead

items.prompt: In Greek mythology, who is the god of wine, theater, festivity, and ritual ecstasy, associated with satyrs, maenads, and the theatrical mask?
items.explanation:
God of wine, theater, and ecstatic ritual in Greek mythology; Roman equivalent is Bacchus
Twice-born: first from his mortal mother Semele (killed by Zeus's glory), then from Zeus's thigh where Zeus sewed the fetus to gestate
His retinue includes satyrs (half-man, half-goat) and maenads ('raving women') who engage in frenzied ritual
Athenian theater (tragedy and comedy) developed from his festivals; the theatrical mask is his symbol
Associated with the concept of destroying normal boundaries and social order through ecstasy and intoxication
The cult of Dionysus was among the most emotionally intense in ancient Greece; his mysteries were celebrated at night
items.cardFront: God of wine, theater, ecstasy; satyrs and maenads

items.prompt: In Greek mythology, who is the god of fire and the forge, the divine blacksmith of the gods, and the only Olympian with a physical disability?
items.explanation:
God of fire, metalworking, and craftsmanship in Greek mythology; Roman equivalent is Vulcan
Lame from birth (or thrown from Olympus and disabled in the fall, depending on the version)
Forged the weapons and palaces of the gods: Achilles's armor, Hermes's winged sandals, Zeus's thunderbolts
Married to Aphrodite, who was famously unfaithful with Ares; Hephaestus trapped them in a golden net
His workshop is associated with volcanoes; the word 'volcano' comes from the Roman name Vulcan
Despite his ugliness, he was the most skilled craftsman in the universe; a symbol of skill triumphing over appearance
items.cardFront: Divine blacksmith; god of fire and forge; lame Olympian

items.prompt: In Greek mythology, who is the messenger of the gods, conductor of souls to the underworld, and god of trade, thieves, and travelers, depicted with winged sandals and a caduceus?
items.explanation:
Messenger of the gods and psychopomp (guide of souls to the underworld) in Greek mythology; Roman equivalent is Mercury
Son of Zeus and the nymph Maia; born in a cave on Mount Cyllene and stole Apollo's cattle on the very day of his birth
Wears winged sandals (talaria) and winged helmet; carries the caduceus (a staff entwined with two serpents)
God of boundaries, travel, commerce, thieves, and eloquence; patron of merchants and messengers
The caduceus (Hermes's staff) is often mistakenly used as a symbol of medicine; the correct medical symbol is the Rod of Asclepius (one serpent)
Also the inventor of the lyre, which he traded to Apollo in exchange for the cattle he had stolen
items.cardFront: Messenger god; caduceus; winged sandals; guide of souls

items.prompt: What is the Roman name for the Greek god of war, Ares?
items.explanation:
Ares (Greek) = Mars (Roman); god of war, bloodshed, and violence
Son of Zeus and Hera; the most warlike but least liked of the Olympians — even his father Zeus called him hateful
Had an affair with Aphrodite (Venus), goddess of love; they were caught in Hephaestus's golden net
Ares was worshipped more in Sparta than Athens; considered brutal and impulsive vs. Athena's strategic warfare
The planet Mars is named after him; the month of March (Martius) also derives from Mars
Mars was considerably more important in Roman religion than Ares was in Greek — as father of Romulus, he was the divine ancestor of Rome
items.cardFront: Ares — god of war (Greek name)

items.prompt: What is the Roman name for the Greek goddess of love and beauty, Aphrodite?
items.explanation:
Aphrodite (Greek) = Venus (Roman); goddess of love, beauty, pleasure, and procreation
Born from the sea foam where the severed genitals of the god Uranus fell; emerged fully-grown near Cyprus
Married to Hephaestus but famous for her affair with Ares; also loved the mortal Adonis
Mother of Eros (Cupid in Roman mythology), the god of love who shoots golden arrows
The Trojan War was triggered in part by Aphrodite: she promised Paris the most beautiful woman (Helen) as a bribe in the Judgment of Paris
*The Birth of Venus* (c. 1484–1486) by Sandro Botticelli is among the most famous paintings in Western art
items.cardFront: Aphrodite — goddess of love and beauty (Greek name)

items.prompt: What is the Roman name for the Greek goddess of the hunt, wilderness, and the moon, Artemis, the twin sister of Apollo?
items.explanation:
Artemis (Greek) = Diana (Roman); goddess of the hunt, wilderness, and the moon
Twin sister of Apollo; both children of Zeus and Leto
Virgin goddess who swore never to marry; punished those who violated her chastity or hunting grounds with extreme harshness
Actaeon the hunter accidentally saw her bathing; she transformed him into a stag and his own hounds tore him apart
Depicted with a bow and arrows, accompanied by a deer or a hunting dog
Worshipped in the great Temple of Artemis at Ephesus — one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
items.cardFront: Artemis — goddess of the hunt, moon; Apollo's twin (Greek name)

items.prompt: Which Olympian god, the same in both Greek and Roman mythology, is the god of the sun, music, poetry, prophecy, and healing, associated with the Oracle at Delphi?
items.explanation:
One of the few Olympian gods with the same name in both Greek and Roman mythology (no Latin equivalent name)
God of the sun, light, music, poetry, truth, prophecy, and healing; twin brother of Artemis
The Oracle at Delphi (the Pythia) spoke Apollo's prophecies; her utterances shaped Greek politics for centuries
Killed the serpent Python at Delphi; the Pythian Games were held in his honor
His lyre was given to him by Hermes (who invented it using a tortoise shell)
Depicted as eternally youthful and beautiful; the 'ideal' of male beauty in Greek sculpture; associated with reason and order as a counterpart to Dionysus's chaos
items.cardFront: God of sun, music, prophecy; Oracle at Delphi (same Greek/Roman name)

items.prompt: What is the Roman name for the Greek goddess of the harvest and agriculture, Demeter, whose daughter's abduction by Hades causes the seasons?
items.explanation:
Demeter (Greek) = Ceres (Roman); goddess of the harvest, grain, and agriculture
Her daughter Persephone was abducted by Hades; Demeter's grief caused all crops to die (winter)
Zeus negotiated Persephone's partial return: she spends 2/3 of the year with Demeter (spring/summer) and 1/3 with Hades (autumn/winter)
The Eleusinian Mysteries — secret initiatory rites held annually near Athens — celebrated Demeter and Persephone
The word 'cereal' derives from Ceres; she held the grain sheaf as her symbol
In Roman religion, Ceres was particularly associated with the plebeian class, whose temple stood on the Aventine Hill
items.cardFront: Demeter — goddess of harvest; Persephone's mother (Greek name)

items.prompt: Which Greek hero sailed to Crete, killed the Minotaur in the Labyrinth using Ariadne's thread to find his way out, and later founded or reformed the city of Athens?
items.explanation:
Athenian hero-king; son of Aegeus (king of Athens) and, by some accounts, also of Poseidon
The Minotaur (half-man, half-bull) lived in a labyrinth built by Daedalus; Athens sent tribute of seven youths and seven maidens to be devoured annually
Ariadne, daughter of King Minos of Crete, fell in love with Theseus and gave him a thread to follow back out; he killed the Minotaur and escaped
Abandoned Ariadne on the island of Naxos; forgot to change his ship's sails from black to white — his father Aegeus, seeing black sails, threw himself into the sea (the Aegean Sea)
Later united the city-states of Attica into a single Athens; credited with founding Athenian democracy
Parallel hero to Heracles (Hercules) in the canon of Greek mythology
items.cardFront: Killed the Minotaur; Ariadne's thread; founded Athens

items.prompt: Which Greek hero, son of Zeus and the mortal Alcmene, completed twelve labors including killing the Nemean Lion, the Lernaean Hydra, and capturing Cerberus?
items.explanation:
Greatest of the Greek heroes; son of Zeus and the mortal Alcmene; known as Hercules in Roman mythology
The Twelve Labors were imposed by King Eurystheus as penance for killing his own family in a fit of madness sent by Hera (who hated him as Zeus's illegitimate son)
Labors include: slaying the Nemean Lion (wore its pelt thereafter), killing the Lernaean Hydra, cleaning the Augean stables, capturing Cerberus (the three-headed dog)
Possessed superhuman strength; his club and lion-skin are his iconic attributes
Died from a poisoned shirt sent by his wife Deianira, who had been tricked into thinking it would ensure his fidelity
Apotheosized (became a god) after death; the only mortal to achieve full Olympian status
items.cardFront: Twelve Labors; superhuman strength; son of Zeus

items.prompt: Which Greek hero, known for his cunning rather than strength, is the protagonist of Homer's *Odyssey* and spent ten years trying to return home to Ithaca after the Trojan War?
items.explanation:
Hero of Homer's *Odyssey* (c. 8th century BC); called Ulysses in Latin/Roman tradition
King of Ithaca; husband of Penelope; father of Telemachus
Trapped by Calypso on her island, blinded the Cyclops Polyphemus (son of Poseidon), navigated Scylla and Charybdis, resisted the Sirens
Devised the Trojan Horse — the stratagem that ended the Trojan War after ten years of siege
Penelope, waiting for his return, unraveled her weaving each night to stall her suitors' demands for marriage
Returned home in disguise and, with Telemachus, killed all 108 suitors in the great hall
His name ('he who causes pain' or 'he who is hated') is fitting — Poseidon prolonged his journey as revenge for blinding Polyphemus
items.cardFront: Ten-year voyage home from Troy; cunning hero; Trojan Horse

items.prompt: Which Greek hero of the Trojan War was the greatest warrior, but was invulnerable everywhere except his heel, where his mother Thetis held him when dipping him in the River Styx?
items.explanation:
Central hero of Homer's *Iliad* (c. 8th century BC); son of the Nereid Thetis and the mortal Peleus
His rage at Agamemnon and subsequent withdrawal from battle — then his grief-driven return after Patroclus is killed — are the *Iliad*'s central arc
Killed Hector (Troy's greatest warrior) to avenge Patroclus; then was killed himself by an arrow in his heel shot by Paris (guided by Apollo)
Thetis dipped him in the Styx to make him invulnerable; the one spot she held — his heel — remained mortal
The 'Achilles tendon' is named after him; an 'Achilles heel' means a hidden fatal weakness
Chose a short glorious life over a long obscure one; told Odysseus in the underworld he would rather be a living serf than king of the dead
items.cardFront: Greatest Trojan War warrior; invulnerable except his heel

items.prompt: Which Greek hero slew the Gorgon Medusa by using a mirrored shield to avoid her petrifying gaze, then rescued the princess Andromeda from a sea monster?
items.explanation:
Son of Zeus and the mortal Danaë; raised on the island of Seriphos
Equipped by the gods: Athena gave him a mirrored shield, Hermes gave winged sandals and the adamantine sickle, the Graeae (three sisters sharing one eye) were tricked into giving him Medusa's location
Mediate's head turned onlookers to stone — Perseus used the reflection in his shield; decapitated her while looking away
From Medusa's neck sprang the winged horse Pegasus and the giant Chrysaor
Rescued Andromeda from the sea monster Cetus (sent by Poseidon at Cassiopeia's boast that Andromeda was more beautiful than the Nereids)
Married Andromeda; founded the city of Mycenae; was the great-grandfather of Heracles
items.cardFront: Slew Medusa with mirrored shield; rescued Andromeda

items.prompt: Which master craftsman built the Labyrinth of Crete, then fashioned wings of feathers and wax for himself and his son Icarus to escape from the island?
items.explanation:
The greatest mortal craftsman in Greek mythology; Athenian, but exiled for killing a nephew out of jealousy of his talent
Built the Labyrinth at the command of King Minos to house the Minotaur; then Minos imprisoned him (and Icarus) to prevent the secret from spreading
He fashioned wings from feathers and beeswax; warned Icarus not to fly too low (sea spray) or too high (sun)
Icarus flew too high; the wax melted and he plunged into the sea — the Icarian Sea (near the island Ikaria) is named for him
Daedalus reached Sicily safely; Minos pursued him and was killed by the daughters of King Cocalus who poured boiling water on him
His name is the origin of the English word 'dedal' (intricate, skillful) and the adjective 'Daedalian'
items.cardFront: Master craftsman; built the Labyrinth; father of Icarus

items.prompt: Which Titan was punished by Zeus for stealing fire from the gods and giving it to humanity — chained to a rock while an eagle ate his liver daily, only for it to regrow each night?
items.explanation:
A Titan (pre-Olympian god) in Greek mythology; name means 'forethought'
Created humanity from clay in some versions; in all versions is humanity's great benefactor and champion against the gods
Zeus had withheld fire from humans; Prometheus stole it from Hephaestus's forge (or from Zeus's chariot) and gave it to mortals
Zeus chained him to Mount Caucasus; an eagle (or vulture) ate his regenerating liver every day
Finally freed by Heracles, who killed the eagle
Mary Shelley subtitled *Frankenstein* (1818) 'The Modern Prometheus'; Percy Shelley wrote *Prometheus Unbound* (1820)
A symbol of revolutionary defiance against oppressive authority and the gift of knowledge to ordinary people
items.cardFront: Stole fire from gods; tortured by eagle; eternal rebel

items.prompt: Which legendary king of Phrygia was granted the power to turn everything he touched into gold by the god Dionysus, only to discover the gift was a curse?
items.explanation:
King of Phrygia in Asia Minor; a historical Phrygian king named Midas existed c. 738–696 BC, though the myth is legendary
Granted his wish by Dionysus as a reward for returning the satyr Silenus; turned food, drink, and his beloved daughter to gold
Dionysus told him to wash in the Pactolus River to undo the curse; the river's sands became rich in gold
A second famous Midas myth: given donkey's ears by Apollo for judging Pan's music superior; his barber whispered the secret into a hole in the ground, from which reeds grew and the wind carried the secret
'The Midas touch' = an ability to make money from everything; the 'golden touch' is a universal metaphor
items.cardFront: King of Phrygia; everything he touched turned to gold
